title: Chapter 16: Julian's Studio
content: The lighthouse stood at the edge of Blackwood Vale like a broken tooth in the mouth of the sea.

I had watched Julian leave the Harbor Gallery at seven, his rolled canvases tucked under one arm, his gait unhurried, confident—the walk of a man who owned every room he entered and every road between them. He'd said something to the gallery director that made her laugh, a bright sound that carried across the parking lot and died in the fog. Then he'd driven east toward the prep site, and I'd waited twenty more minutes in the cold, counting my heartbeats, before I crossed the salt-grass toward the lighthouse.

The fog wrapped around it like a glove. I couldn't see the top from the base—just the white curve of the tower dissolving into grey nothing, as if the structure simply stopped existing above the second floor. The converted lighthouse. Julian's live-work space. His sanctum.
I told myself I was investigating. I told myself I needed to understand my enemy.
I didn't tell myself that I was already inside his orbit and had been since the night he'd looked at me across the Buena kitchen and known exactly who I was.

The ground-floor door was locked—a heavy iron thing with a salt-rusted deadbolt. But the side entrance, the one that had once been the lighthouse keeper's supply hatch, yielded to a hairpin and patience. I slipped through into a narrow corridor that smelled of linseed oil and brine. The combination was almost pleasant at first—bohemian, artistic—until the smell deepened, and I realized the salt wasn't just from the sea. It was from preserve. From curing. From things kept far past their natural span.
The studio opened before me like a confession.

Julian's taste was, as always, impeccable. That was the most dangerous thing about him. A sloppy predator you can condemn. A beautiful one seduces you into the cage. The main room was a converted galley space with vaulted ceilings and enormous north-facing windows that would have let in clear light if the fog hadn't swallowed the world. His working canvases lined the walls—landscapes of the Vale, abstracts in deep greens and blacks that seemed to move when I wasn't looking directly at them. A potter's wheel sat unused in the corner, draped in canvas. His couch was Danish modern, probably authentic, the kind of furniture that said I have money and I have time and I have no doubt that I deserve both.

A drafting table held sketches. I flipped through them quickly—studies of the harbor, the cliffs, the sea stack. Competent. Lovely, even. The hand of a man who had spent years, maybe decades, learning to see.
But the main room was a performance. I knew it the way you know a stage set. Everything visible was meant to be seen. And what Julian wanted seen was Julian Cross: Artist. Gentleman. Creator.
Not collector.

I found the back room behind a bookshelf that swung on hidden hinges. Of course it did. Of course there was a hidden room. The reveal was so theatrical that I almost laughed—would have laughed, if the air inside hadn't stolen the breath from my throat.

The paintings covered every wall. Floor to ceiling. Hanging in rows, some framed, some tacked directly to the plaster, some leaning against each other in makeshift racks that groaned under the weight of so much history.
They were all portraits.
They were all Buena women.

Maren. She was the first I recognized—the high cheekbones, the dark cloud of hair, the ivy curling across her lips. Not decorative. Growing *out* of her mouth, her throat, her lungs. The painter had captured the moment the green choked her: her eyes half-lidded, her fingers clawing at her neck, the vines bursting through the skin of her chest like something planted and harvested. The year at the bottom of the frame read 1923.

Cordelia. 1947. She sat at a ledger, columns of figures in her careful hand. But the numbers weren't numbers—they were names. Buena names. All the women who had come before her, all the women who would come after, and Cordelia's own name written in red at the bottom of the final column, the sum that balanced the books. Her face was serene. The face of a woman who knew the cost and paid it anyway.

Iris. 1961. Empty arms. That was the whole painting. Iris standing in a nursery where the crib was filled with black water. Her arms cradled air. Her mouth was open in a soundless scream that I could almost hear—the high, animal keen of a woman whose child had been taken by the curse before it could draw its first breath.

Hazel. 1979. She held a paintbrush in one hand, and the other hand bore a signature that wasn't hers. Another man's name. The painting had captured the theft mid-act—her art leaving her body and entering his, the signature writing itself across her skin like a brand.

Vivienne. 1995. She was burning. Not in fire—in *memory*. Her features were melting, running like wax, the edges of her becoming indistinct. Even in the portrait, you could see her losing coherence, her selfhood smearing outward into nothing. By the time Julian had finished the painting, there had been nothing left of Vivienne but the shape of a woman who used to be someone.

Nadine. 2012. My mother. She was on her knees in the garden, hands buried in soil. But below the soil line, her hands weren't hands anymore. They were roots, thick and gnarled, plunging into the earth. The garden was feeding on her, and she was letting it. Her face held an expression I recognized—the Buena resignation. The look that said: *I know what I am. I know what I'm for.*
Six paintings. Six women. Six trophies.
I touched my own face without thinking—the instinct of a woman checking that she's still there.
Then I saw the seventh.
It stood on an easel in the center of the room, positioned so that the hidden north-light lamp fell directly on the canvas. The painting was smaller than the others. Intimate. A portrait in progress.
It was me.

My body, my hands, my shape—the curve of my shoulders, the way I hold my arms close when I'm frightened, the particular angle of my wrists. He'd captured my posture with the precision of a man who had been watching me for months. Or years. Or lifetimes. The fabric of my clothes was rendered in obsessive detail—the worn hem of my jacket, the stain on my left cuff that I thought no one had noticed.
But the painting had no face.
Just smooth, blank canvas where my features should have been. An oval of emptiness. My identity, unpainted. Unmade.
He was painting my soul out.

The horror of it hit me in waves. First the intellectual understanding—*he's removing my face while I still wear it*—and then the visceral, bodily wave that followed, the cold wash of recognition that I was looking at a process, not a product. The painting was unfinished. Which meant the consumption was ongoing. The face was the final seal. Once he filled in my features, I would belong to him the way the others had belonged to him. My identity would transfer from my body to his canvas, and what remained of me would be a shell. A shape. A woman-shaped absence, like Vivienne melting, like Nadine rooting into the dark.
I reached out to touch the blank space where my face should have been.

My fingers stopped an inch from the canvas. I couldn't do it. Some part of me knew—instinctively, the way you know not to touch a hot iron—that contact would accelerate the process. That the painting was a conduit, and my touch would open it wider.
I stepped back.
I heard footsteps.

The sound came from the main room—boots on hardwood, unhurried, the rhythm of a man returning to a space that belonged to him. The studio door closed with a soft click. Then the bookshelf hinge groaned.

I had seconds. I grabbed the largest canvas I could find—a landscape, four feet tall—and slid behind it, pressing myself into the corner between the wall and a rack of framed works. My heart slammed so hard I was certain he could hear it. I tasted copper—my own lip, bitten through—and the metallic tang mixed with the turpentine air until I couldn't tell where my blood ended and his solvents began.
Julian entered the back room.
He didn't look around. Didn't search. He walked directly to the easel, directly to my portrait, and stood before it the way a man stands before a meal.

He picked up a brush from the tray beside the easel. I watched his hand—long fingers, steady, the confident grip of someone who had done this many times before. He dipped the brush into a color I couldn't identify. Something between flesh and shadow.
And he began to work on the face.
He hummed while he painted. A tune I didn't recognize—old, modal, something between a lullaby and a hymn. The sound was content. Sated. Like a man feeding.
The brush touched the canvas.
I felt it.

A pull at my sense of self. Not physical—not pain, exactly. More like thinning. Like the edges of me were becoming less dense, my identity *diluting*—becoming weaker, the essential Aurelia-ness of me becoming water. My skin prickled. My vision blurred at the periphery. I pressed my hand against the wall to steady myself and felt my palm go through the plaster as if my solidity were optional.
The brush made another stroke.

I felt my name thin. Felt the specific weight of my memories—my childhood, my mother's hands, the kitchen in the Buena house—become lighter. As if Julian were reaching across the room and pulling threads out of the fabric of who I was, one by one, wrapping them around his brush, transferring them to the canvas where they would exist at *his* pleasure, not mine.
The brush moved again. And again. Each stroke a theft. Each stroke a transfer.
I couldn't stay.

I moved. Slowly, fighting the pull of the conduit, fighting the gravity of the painting that wanted to hold me in place while it consumed me. My legs were heavy—not with fear, but with *absence*. Parts of me were already in the canvas. I could feel them missing, like phantom limbs, like the memory of a tooth that's been pulled.

The window. The back room had a window—a narrow porthole from the lighthouse's original architecture. I reached it in four steps that felt like four miles. The latch was rusted. I pulled. My hands were shaking. Or—they were less than shaking. They were becoming less substantial.
The latch gave.
Cold night air hit my face. Salt. Fog. The cliff dropped away below the window in a tumble of rock and black water. I didn't think. I climbed through.

The cliff path was narrow and slick with mist. I half-climbed, half-fell down it, my shoes skidding on wet stone, my hands tearing on scrub grass that clung to the rock face. The fog swallowed me. I let it. I wanted to be swallowed. I wanted to be invisible, formless, everything that Julian's painting couldn't capture.
I ran.

The fog was inside my lungs—thick and wet and cold, tasting of brine and something older, something that had been in this vale since before the town, before the lighthouse, before the Buena women began dying. I ran until my chest burned and my legs screamed and I couldn't hear anything behind me but the crash of the sea.
Julian didn't follow.

He was absorbed in the painting. I could still feel him working—distant now, fainter, like a radio signal losing strength. But there. The pull at my edges, the slow drain of identity, thinner now but not gone. Not stopped.
And then his voice, carrying across the fog as if the mist itself were his medium:
"Did you find what you were looking for?"
Cold. Amused. The voice of a man who had known I was there the entire time.
I didn't stop running. I didn't answer. But the question stayed with me, lodged under my ribs like a splinter.
*Did you find what you were looking for?*
He'd let me see. He'd wanted me to know.
It wasn't a revelation. It was a threat.

I made it home. The Buena house was dark—no one waiting up, no one to see me stumble through the kitchen door with fog in my hair and terror in my throat. I locked the door behind me. Checked the windows. Checked them again.
Then I sat at the kitchen table with my grandmother's journal open before me and wrote until my hand cramped.

*He's been hunting my family for 260 years. Or the curse has been sending versions of him. I don't know which is worse—a monster who chooses, or a system that produces monsters. Either way, the paintings are real. He's EATEN six women and I'm the seventh course. The painting doesn't have my face yet.*
*But it WILL.*
